content.text: Deploying AI agents into production reveals a class of operational challenges that traditional application monitoring does not address: nondeterministic behavior, multi-step LLM call chains, tool invocations, variable token costs, and emergent failure modes that only appear during real sessions. AgentOps is an observability and management platform built specifically for this environment.

AgentOps is an open-source Python SDK paired with a hosted backend that provides end-to-end observability, session recording, cost tracking, and debugging tooling for AI agent applications. It wraps LLM provider clients and agent frameworks at the SDK level, capturing every LLM call, tool invocation, memory read, and error that occurs during a session without requiring manual instrumentation of each individual call.

in AgentOps terminology corresponds to one full agent run — from the moment an agent is initialized to the point it returns a final answer or terminates. During that session, the SDK records a timestamped event stream that includes prompt text, completion text, token counts, model parameters, tool call inputs and outputs, latency, and any exceptions. This event stream is shipped to the AgentOps backend in real time and can be replayed, filtered, and analyzed from the web dashboard at

AgentOps supports the most widely used LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Mistral) and agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, LlamaIndex) through auto-patching: the SDK monkey-patches the provider SDK's completion methods at import time so that instrumentation is active for every call in the process without code changes to existing agent logic.

Traditional APM tools (Datadog, New Relic, Prometheus) were designed for request-response services with deterministic behavior and structured metrics. An AI agent session looks nothing like an HTTP request: it may span dozens of LLM calls, branch conditionally based on model output, retry on parse failures, invoke external tools, and accumulate cost over minutes rather than milliseconds. Standard tools surface latency percentiles and error rates but cannot answer the questions that matter most when an agent misbehaves.

Without agent-specific observability, diagnosing a production failure requires reconstructing what happened from fragmented application logs. This process is slow, error-prone, and often incomplete because logs capture application-level events but not the full prompt-completion pairs that drove the agent's decisions. The failure might occur at step 7 of a 12-step agent loop, triggered by an ambiguous LLM output at step 4 — a chain of causation that is invisible in a flat log.

AgentOps closes this gap by making the full session replay — every prompt, completion, tool call, and state transition — available as a structured, searchable artifact. Teams can reproduce any session exactly as it occurred, identify the LLM call that produced unexpected output, and verify that a fix eliminates the failure pattern before deploying to production.
Every agent run is recorded as a complete, replayable trace. Debugging a reported failure means opening the session in the dashboard and stepping through each event — no log reconstruction needed.

Token counts are captured per call and aggregated per session. Cost is computed using current provider pricing and displayed in the dashboard, making it straightforward to identify expensive agent patterns and set budget alerts.
Calling agentops.init() at the top of the entry point is sufficient to capture all LLM calls. No decorator on every function, no manual span creation, no changes to existing agent code.
Native integrations for LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, and LlamaIndex add agent-level context (agent name, role, sub-agent hierarchy) to the raw LLM events, making multi-agent traces navigable.
For multi-agent systems, the dashboard renders the agent interaction graph — which agent called which sub-agent, what was delegated, and what each returned — as a visual flow diagram.

Exceptions raised during a session are captured with full stack traces and linked to the LLM call or tool invocation that preceded them. Sessions can be tagged (e.g., success, failure, needs_review) for systematic analysis.

The AgentOps Python SDK exposes methods to export session data, enabling custom analytics pipelines, fine-tuning dataset creation from successful sessions, and integration with internal data warehouses.

The SDK ships prompt text, completion text, and tool inputs/outputs to the AgentOps backend. For applications handling regulated data (PII, PHI, financial records), this is a compliance concern. AgentOps offers a self-hosted deployment option, but it requires operating the backend infrastructure.

The SDK adds a thin synchronous wrapper around each LLM call to capture timing and payload. The overhead is typically under 1 ms per call, but in latency-critical paths this should be measured rather than assumed negligible.

As of mid-2025, the AgentOps SDK is Python-only. TypeScript/JavaScript agents require a REST API integration rather than the native SDK, which provides fewer automatic captures and requires more manual instrumentation.

Agents that generate very long completions or call LLMs thousands of times per session produce large event payloads. The free tier caps storage; teams running high-volume agents must budget for the paid plan.

Session replay shows what happened but cannot re-execute the session with different parameters. For evaluating whether a code change would have produced a different outcome, a separate evaluation harness is needed.

Auto-patching works by hooking into specific internal methods of LLM provider SDKs. Major version updates to openai or anthropic packages occasionally break the patch points until AgentOps releases a compatible SDK version.
is called, the SDK registers itself as a listener by monkey-patching the

methods on all detected LLM provider clients in the Python process. It also patches tool-calling wrappers in supported frameworks. From this point on, every call that flows through the patched methods is intercepted before and after execution.
Before the call, AgentOps records the
(the full messages list for chat models), the model name, temperature, and any other parameters. After the call completes, it records the
, the token usage reported by the provider (prompt tokens, completion tokens, total tokens), and the wall-clock latency. This pre/post pair is called an

. When an agent invokes a function (web search, code execution, database query), the SDK records the function name, the arguments passed to it, the return value, and whether an exception was raised. The action event is linked to the LLM event that generated the tool call, preserving the causal chain.
All events within a session share a

is called explicitly). The SDK buffers events in memory and flushes them to the AgentOps backend over HTTPS in small batches, typically every few seconds or when a configurable buffer size is reached. A final flush is triggered when
is called or the process exits.

On the backend, events are stored against the session ID. The backend computes derived metrics: total tokens, estimated cost (using a pricing table for each provider and model), session duration, number of LLM calls, tool call success rate, and error count. These aggregates power the dashboard views and can be queried via the AgentOps REST API.

attrs.alt: AgentOps observability architecture: AI Agent Application connects to AgentOps SDK which sends events over HTTPS to the AgentOps Backend (session collector, event processor, cost calculator, error detector), data is stored (sessions, LLM event traces, cost/token data, error logs, agent graphs), and surfaced in the Dashboard (session replay, trace timeline, cost analytics, error alerts, agent graph view)

content.text: The SDK layer runs in-process alongside the agent application. There is no sidecar, proxy, or network hop between the agent and the SDK — all interception is done via Python method patching in the same process. The only external communication is the HTTPS flush from the SDK to the AgentOps backend, which is asynchronous and does not block the agent's execution path.

The backend is a stateless HTTP service backed by a persistent store. Each session's event log is append-only: events are written once and never mutated, which ensures the audit trail is tamper-evident. The dashboard queries are served from a read replica, keeping the write path (event ingestion) decoupled from the read path (analytics and replay).

AgentOps is appropriate whenever an AI agent application moves beyond prototype stage and needs production-quality debugging, cost visibility, or behavioral auditing. Specific scenarios where it provides clear value:
Multi-step agents with unpredictable failure modes:
When an agent can fail at any of ten LLM calls and the failure reason varies per session, session replay is the only practical debugging approach.

Any agent that makes repeated LLM calls benefits from per-session and per-call cost visibility. Without it, cost anomalies (agent stuck in a loop, unexpectedly long completions) are discovered only on the monthly invoice.
Orchestrator-subagent architectures quickly become opaque. AgentOps' agent graph view makes the delegation structure and each agent's contribution to the final answer visible.

Applications that must demonstrate what the AI system did during a session (healthcare assistants, financial advisory tools) benefit from the tamper-evident event log that AgentOps maintains per session.
Successful sessions captured by AgentOps can be exported as prompt-completion pairs and used as training data for fine-tuning smaller models to replicate agent behavior at lower cost.

AgentOps is less suitable for single-call LLM wrappers where standard APM logging is sufficient; applications where the prompt data is too sensitive to send to any external service and self-hosting the backend is not feasible; or non-Python agent stacks where the SDK's auto-instrumentation does not apply.
The following steps integrate AgentOps into a new or existing Python agent application.
Run pip install agentops and add it to the project's requirements file.
Create an account at app.agentops.ai and copy the API key from the Settings page.

Call agentops.init(api_key=os.environ['AGENTOPS_API_KEY']) as early as possible in the entry point — before any LLM provider clients are created or any agent code runs. This ensures the monkey-patching covers every subsequent call.
Decorate agent classes or functions with @agentops.track_agent(name='...') to associate events with named agents in the dashboard. Required for multi-agent graph visualization.
Call agentops.end_session('Success') or agentops.end_session('Fail', end_state_reason='...') when the agent finishes. This triggers the final event flush and stamps the session with an outcome label.
Run the agent once and open app.agentops.ai. The session should appear within a few seconds with all LLM calls, token counts, and cost visible.
Set cost-per-session thresholds or error-rate triggers in the dashboard to receive email or webhook notifications when agent behavior exceeds expected parameters.
Minimal integration with the OpenAI SDK

import os
import agentops
from openai import OpenAI

# Initialize AgentOps before creating the OpenAI client.
# The SDK patches openai.chat.completions.create automatically.
agentops.init(api_key=os.environ["AGENTOPS_API_KEY"])

client = OpenAI()

def answer_question(question: str) -> str:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
            {"role": "user", "content": question},
        ],
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

result = answer_question("What is the capital of France?")
print(result)

# End the session with an outcome label.
# The session is now visible in app.agentops.ai with token counts and cost.
agentops.end_session("Success")
Multi-step research agent with tool tracking
Explicit per-run session management for batch processing

Consider a customer-support automation system that handles inbound support tickets. An LLM agent reads each ticket, decides whether it can answer directly or needs to escalate, optionally queries a knowledge base, and then either drafts a response or creates a follow-up task. The system processes several hundred tickets per day across a pool of parallel agent workers.

Without observability, the engineering team learns about problems from support managers reporting that the agent is giving wrong answers or escalating unnecessarily. With AgentOps, each ticket becomes a session tagged with the ticket ID, category, and outcome. The dashboard reveals that a specific ticket category (billing disputes) has a 40% escalation rate versus 8% for other categories, and that the average token spend per billing ticket is three times the mean — indicating the agent is looping through knowledge base lookups without finding a match.
Each session in the dashboard shows the exact LLM calls made, the knowledge base queries attempted, the tool return values, the token cost, and the final outcome. Sessions tagged

can be filtered to a dedicated view, making the failure pattern immediately visible without writing custom log queries. The average cost per session, visible in the aggregated analytics panel, quantifies the economic impact of the loop before and after the fix is deployed.
Calling agentops.init() after importing the LLM client:

The SDK patches provider clients at init time. If OpenAI or Anthropic clients are instantiated before init(), those instances are not patched and their calls are not captured. Always call agentops.init() at the very top of the entry point before any other imports that create LLM clients.

If end_session() is never called, the SDK flushes remaining events at process exit, but the session is stamped as 'Indeterminate' rather than 'Success' or 'Fail'. Dashboard filtering by outcome becomes unreliable. Wrap agent execution in try/finally to ensure end_session() always runs.
Using the same session across multiple independent agent runs:

A single agentops.init() call opens one session. If the same process runs multiple unrelated agent jobs in sequence, all their events appear in one session in the dashboard, making per-job analysis impossible. Use start_session() / end_session() explicitly to create per-run sessions.
Relying on AgentOps as the primary error handler:

AgentOps captures exceptions and makes them visible in the dashboard, but it does not suppress them or provide retry logic. Error visibility and error recovery are separate concerns. Implement retry and fallback logic in the agent, and use AgentOps for post-mortem analysis.
Sending raw PII to the hosted backend without review:

The SDK captures full prompt and completion text. If users can inject arbitrary text (support tickets, chat messages), PII may be captured. Review data sensitivity before enabling AgentOps in a production environment that handles regulated data.

Because the SDK patches LLM provider internals, a minor SDK update can change patching behavior. Pin agentops==X.Y.Z in requirements.txt and test SDK upgrades in staging before deploying to production.
Store the API key in an environment variable:
Never hard-code the AgentOps API key in source. Read it from the environment (os.environ['AGENTOPS_API_KEY']) and add it to the CI/CD secret store.

Pass tags=['env:production', 'version:1.4.2', 'model:gpt-4o'] to agentops.init() or start_session(). Tags enable cohort-level analysis (compare v1.3 vs v1.4 session cost) without custom analytics code.
Use explicit session management for concurrent workloads:

When multiple agent runs execute concurrently (multiprocessing or async tasks), use agentops.start_session() to obtain a session handle and call end_session() on that handle. This associates events with the correct session even when calls from different tasks interleave.
End sessions in a finally block:

Wrap the agent's main execution in try/except/finally. In the finally block, call agentops.end_session('Success' if not failed else 'Fail'). This guarantees the session is always closed with an accurate outcome, even when an unhandled exception terminates the run.
Record custom events for business-logic milestones:

Use agentops.record(ActionEvent(action_type='document_retrieved', returns={...})) to capture application-level events that are not LLM calls but are important for debugging — knowledge base lookups, cache hits, external API calls.
Filter sensitive fields before they reach the LLM:

Pre-process prompt text to remove fields that should not leave the service boundary (API keys, passwords, SSNs) before they reach the LLM call that AgentOps will capture. The best time to sanitize is before the data enters the prompt, not after it has already been sent to the model.
Use the self-hosted deployment for regulated environments:

The AgentOps server is open source. For environments where data cannot leave the organization's infrastructure, deploy the backend on internal infrastructure and point the SDK to the internal endpoint via the endpoint parameter of agentops.init().

The AgentOps SDK's overhead on each LLM call is dominated by payload serialization and the asynchronous HTTPS flush, not by the interception itself. In practice, the serialization adds 0.5 to 2 milliseconds per event on a modern CPU, and the flush runs on a background thread so it does not block the LLM call's return path. For most agents, LLM call latency (200 ms to several seconds) dwarfs SDK overhead by three or four orders of magnitude.

The SDK buffers events in memory and flushes in batches. The default batch size is 10 events or 3 seconds, whichever comes first. This means a chatty agent that makes 50 LLM calls in quick succession produces approximately 5 HTTPS requests rather than 50, keeping network overhead low. The buffer is configurable: increase batch size for high-throughput batch agents, decrease max_wait_time to minimize the risk of losing events if the process is killed abruptly.

For high-volume deployments (hundreds of concurrent agent sessions), the AgentOps hosted backend handles horizontal scaling transparently. For self-hosted deployments, the backend is a stateless service fronted by a load balancer, and the storage layer (PostgreSQL-compatible) can be scaled independently. Plan for storage proportional to the event volume: a typical 10-step agent session with medium-length completions produces 50 to 100 KB of event data.

Session replay queries are indexed by session ID, tags, and time range. Filtering a large session history (millions of sessions) by tag is fast because the index covers the tags column. Full-text search over prompt and completion content is slower and not recommended as a routine analytics operation — export the data to a columnar store (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) for ad-hoc analysis at scale.
Session recording, LLM patching, event flush
pip install agentops — the primary package
agentops.init() auto-instruments via LangChain callback system
pip install 'agentops[crewai]' — agents appear as named entities in graphs
pip install 'agentops[autogen]' — conversation rounds captured as events
agentops.init() patches LlamaIndex LLM wrappers automatically
Auto-patched at agentops.init() — no additional config
Auto-patched at agentops.init() — no additional config
Bearer token auth — export sessions as JSON for custom analytics
app.agentops.ai — no code changes, data flows automatically

The core differentiator of AgentOps versus generic tracing tools (OpenTelemetry, Datadog) is agent-native semantics: sessions, action events, agent names, and outcome labels are first-class concepts rather than custom attributes bolted onto generic spans. LangSmith provides similar functionality but is tightly coupled to the LangChain ecosystem; AgentOps works across frameworks. Helicone operates as a proxy rather than an SDK, which avoids in-process patching but requires routing all LLM traffic through an additional network hop and does not capture tool invocations or agent-level context.

The AgentOps SDK communicates with the backend exclusively over TLS 1.2+. The API key is transmitted as a bearer token in the Authorization header of each batch flush. Keys can be rotated from the dashboard without redeploying agents; the SDK will receive a 401 on the next flush and log a warning, after which it stops sending data until reinitialized with the new key.

Because the SDK captures prompt and completion text, it has access to any data that flows through the LLM — including user inputs, retrieved documents, and tool outputs. Organizations in regulated industries should review the AgentOps privacy policy and data processing agreement before enabling the hosted backend with production data. The self-hosted deployment option allows all event data to remain within the organization's infrastructure, eliminating the third-party data transfer risk.

The SDK is designed to be non-critical to agent execution: if the event flush fails (network outage, backend unreachable), the failure is logged but the agent continues. Events that cannot be flushed are discarded after a configurable number of retries. This means a backend outage degrades observability but does not break the agent. For compliance use cases where the audit log must be guaranteed, implement a secondary persistence path (write events to a local database before flushing to AgentOps) rather than relying solely on the SDK's in-memory buffer.

For multi-tenant applications where different end users' sessions must be isolated, use separate AgentOps sessions per user and never mix user data within a single session. The tags mechanism enforces per-user namespacing: tag each session with
, and use the AgentOps API's filtering capabilities to restrict which sessions each team member can access based on tag scope.
AgentOps captures every LLM call, tool invocation, and error as a timestamped event stream without manual instrumentation — a single
call instruments the entire agent process.
maps one-to-one to one agent run; ending it with
stamps the outcome and triggers the final event flush.
is the primary debugging primitive: every prompt, completion, tool call, and error in a failing session can be stepped through in the dashboard without log reconstruction.
Token cost is tracked per call and per session
using provider pricing tables, making it practical to identify expensive agent patterns and set budget alerts before they appear on an invoice.
For multi-agent systems, annotate agents with
to produce an agent interaction graph showing delegation chains and each agent's contribution.
: flush failures are logged but do not interrupt the agent, making AgentOps safe to enable in production without adding a new failure mode.
Prompt and completion text leaves the process — review data sensitivity before enabling the hosted backend with regulated data, and use the
